Authors: Chong-Guo He; Yu-Zhao Li; Daniel J. Kontak; Xiao-Ye Jin; Shi-Guang Du; Zheng Xu; Huan Tao; Hang-Jin Jiang; Jian-Wei Li

Journal: Economic Geology

DOI: 10.5382/econgeo.5242

Relevance: 0.628 (must_read)

Abstract The general lack of absolute ages for most Au deposits in the well-endowed West Qinling orogen (central China) has hampered establishment of the exact temporal framework of regional Au mineralization during the geotectonic evolution of this orogen. This study presents new in situ U-Pb ages on hydrothermal monazite in native gold-bearing ores from three world-class Au deposits (Liba, Baguamiao, and Shuangwang) in the West Qinling orogen. The dating results indicate that these deposits formed synchronously at ca. 220 Ma. In all cases, these ages overlap with new U-Pb dates for felsic and mafic magmatism in the deposit settings. Our new age data, when combined with independent geologic evidence and previous geochronological studies, suggest orogen-wide Au mineralization was controlled by the Triassic orogenic processes involving westward diachronous subduction and closure of the Mianlue ocean and subsequent oblique continental convergence of the Yangtze craton and the South Qinling terrane. During ca. 250 to 225 Ma, Au mineralization in the western part of the West Qinling orogen is attributed to oceanic subduction, whereas in the eastern part it is related to incipient continental collision. At ca. 225 to 210 Ma, continental collision culminated in the final formation of the West Qinling orogen, during which slab tearing induced upwelling of the asthenosphere and melting of lithospheric mantle and continental crust, as is reflected by the widespread granitic magmatism and synchronous mafic dikes. These latter events facilitated extensive crustal fluid flow that generated the significant Au endowment in the West Qinling orogen as represented by the dated three deposits.

item.title: ẟ88/86Sr and 87Sr/86Sr variability in global carbonatites: Implications for the role of mineralogy, hydrothermal alteration, and crustal recycling
item.dc:creator: Soumyaroop Bhattacharya
Ramananda Chakrabarti
item.description: We report ẟ88/86Sr values (relative to NIST SRM 987), measured using a double-spike thermal ionization mass spectrometry (DS-TIMS) technique, and 87Sr/86Sr of twenty-one whole rock (WR) globally distributed carbonatites, whose eruption ages range from Proterozoic to recent. Most carbonatites show ẟ88/86Sr values (0.22–0.33 ‰) which broadly overlap with the estimated ẟ88/86Sr value of the bulk silicate Earth (BSE, 0.27 ± 0.05 ‰) (Moynier et al., 2010). Three carbonatite samples from Ambadongar, India (0.36–0.40 ‰) along with a fluorspar from Ambadongar (1.13 ‰), show higher ẟ88/86Sr values caused by secondary hydrothermal processes, supported by presence of hydrothermal minerals. Silicate igneous rocks (alkali basalts) from Phenai Mata, genetically linked to the Ambadongar carbonatites, show slightly lower ẟ88/86Sr values (0.21–0.26 ‰) that most-likely result from higher abundances of plagioclase. In contrast to the ẟ88/86Sr values, 87Sr/86Sr ratios in these carbonatites show significant variation (0.702007–0.710425), with younger carbonatites showing more radiogenic ratios. The ẟ88/86Sr values in global carbonatites cannot be explained by partial melting, fractional crystallization or liquid immiscibility. The BSE-like ẟ88/86Sr values are best explained by combined contributions of different components of the recycled lithosphere (marine carbonates, unaltered oceanic crust and altered oceanic crust) and their interaction with the upper mantle. A Monte Carlo simulation was performed using published values of concentrations and isotopic compositions of these lithospheric endmembers as well as the upper mantle by varying the contributions of these lithospheric components. The modelled (median Rmix) ẟ88/86Sr value of the subducting lithosphere overlaps with that of the BSE but the modelled 87Sr/86Sr is more radiogenic than the depleted mantle or bulk silicate Earth (CHUR), both consistent with our measurements. Published: 2026-06-04T23:41:07+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.612 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Soumyaroop Bhattacharya; Ramananda Chakrabarti

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123555

Relevance: 0.612 (must_read)

We report ẟ88/86Sr values (relative to NIST SRM 987), measured using a double-spike thermal ionization mass spectrometry (DS-TIMS) technique, and 87Sr/86Sr of twenty-one whole rock (WR) globally distributed carbonatites, whose eruption ages range from Proterozoic to recent. Most carbonatites show ẟ88/86Sr values (0.22–0.33 ‰) which broadly overlap with the estimated ẟ88/86Sr value of the bulk silicate Earth (BSE, 0.27 ± 0.05 ‰) (Moynier et al., 2010). Three carbonatite samples from Ambadongar, India (0.36–0.40 ‰) along with a fluorspar from Ambadongar (1.13 ‰), show higher ẟ88/86Sr values caused by secondary hydrothermal processes, supported by presence of hydrothermal minerals. Silicate igneous rocks (alkali basalts) from Phenai Mata, genetically linked to the Ambadongar carbonatites, show slightly lower ẟ88/86Sr values (0.21–0.26 ‰) that most-likely result from higher abundances of plagioclase. In contrast to the ẟ88/86Sr values, 87Sr/86Sr ratios in these carbonatites show significant variation (0.702007–0.710425), with younger carbonatites showing more radiogenic ratios. The ẟ88/86Sr values in global carbonatites cannot be explained by partial melting, fractional crystallization or liquid immiscibility. The BSE-like ẟ88/86Sr values are best explained by combined contributions of different components of the recycled lithosphere (marine carbonates, unaltered oceanic crust and altered oceanic crust) and their interaction with the upper mantle. A Monte Carlo simulation was performed using published values of concentrations and isotopic compositions of these lithospheric endmembers as well as the upper mantle by varying the contributions of these lithospheric components. The modelled (median Rmix) ẟ88/86Sr value of the subducting lithosphere overlaps with that of the BSE but the modelled 87Sr/86Sr is more radiogenic than the depleted mantle or bulk silicate Earth (CHUR), both consistent with our measurements.

item.title: Late Paleozoic (ca. 300 Ma) eclogite belt on Hainan Island, South China, probably related to the Paleo-Pacific subduction system
item.dc:creator: Xiaochun Liu
Juan Hu
Longyao Chen
Guanggao Zheng
Jianhua Li
Yuan Tian
Qiao Zhang
Qiuli Li
item.description: Hainan Island in South China lies at the intersection of the Paleo-Tethyan and Paleo-Pacific tectonic regimes. The discovery of Carboniferous oceanic-type eclogites at Chaotanbi on northeastern Hainan Island offers a novel perspective for elucidating the early tectonic evolution of the Southeast Asian continent. Recently, new eclogite exposures have been recognized at Gongmiao and Yadunling, ∼70−100 km southwest of the Chaotanbi eclogites. These eclogites occur as kilometer-scale tectonic lenses in the Huangzhuling Complex, which predominantly comprises Late Devonian gneissic granites with subordinate Silurian clastic metasedimentary rocks. The Gongmiao eclogites generally preserve a primary mineral assemblage of garnet + omphacite + quartz + (rutile + ilmenite) ± amphibole ± zoisite or allanite, with rare kyanite or phengite. Peak pressure-temperature conditions during eclogite-facies metamorphism are estimated at 750−830 °C and 18.5−21.2 kbar, followed by decompressional cooling through amphibolite-facies (690−740 °C and 8.6−12.6 kbar) to greenschist- and prehnite-pumpellyite−facies conditions. The Yadunling eclogites consist mainly of garnet + omphacite (completely symplectized in the matrix) + amphibole + quartz + (rutile + ilmenite) ± epidote ± calcite. They record a metamorphic history from prograde stage at 490−540 °C and 10.7−14.0 kbar to peak stage at 620−700 °C and 16.2−17.9 kbar, followed by near-isothermal decompression (620−670 °C and 8.7−12.7 kbar) and subsequent cooling to greenschist-facies conditions. Ion microprobe zircon U-Pb ages constrain eclogite protoliths to 359 ± 2 Ma (Gongmiao) and metamorphism to 310−295 Ma, with rutile cooling ages of 295−285 Ma. Trace element geochemical analyses indicate that the Gongmiao eclogites have an island arc geochemical signature, with bulk-rock initial εNd values of −1.0 to +2.7 and an average zircon initial εHf value of +11.1, whereas the Yadunling eclogites display affinities consistent with normal mid-ocean-ridge basalt, with initial εNd values of +5.5 to +6.2. These data suggest that their protoliths correspond to arc-type gabbros and oceanic basalts, respectively, formed in a backarc basin environment. Minor siliceous rocks (with terrigenous detritus) within or adjacent to the Yadunling eclogites likely experienced synchronous metamorphism and deformation along with the eclogites. However, the widespread gneissic granites and clastic metasedimentary rocks underwent only low-grade metamorphism at ca. 285 Ma. This implies that the eclogites were tectonically juxtaposed with these country rocks during their exhumation. The coeval Gongmiao, Yadunling, and Chaotanbi eclogites define a >100 km NE-SW−trending medium- to high-temperature eclogite belt on eastern Hainan Island. The formation of this eclogite belt is inferred to be related to the opening and closure of a backarc basin and subsequent arc-continent collision, which are induced by the retreating to advancing subduction of the western Paleo-Pacific oceanic lithosphere during the late Paleozoic. Published: 2026-06-04T19:23:29+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.610 (consider)

Relevance: 0.610 (consider)

Hainan Island in South China lies at the intersection of the Paleo-Tethyan and Paleo-Pacific tectonic regimes. The discovery of Carboniferous oceanic-type eclogites at Chaotanbi on northeastern Hainan Island offers a novel perspective for elucidating the early tectonic evolution of the Southeast Asian continent. Recently, new eclogite exposures have been recognized at Gongmiao and Yadunling, ∼70−100 km southwest of the Chaotanbi eclogites. These eclogites occur as kilometer-scale tectonic lenses in the Huangzhuling Complex, which predominantly comprises Late Devonian gneissic granites with subordinate Silurian clastic metasedimentary rocks. The Gongmiao eclogites generally preserve a primary mineral assemblage of garnet + omphacite + quartz + (rutile + ilmenite) ± amphibole ± zoisite or allanite, with rare kyanite or phengite. Peak pressure-temperature conditions during eclogite-facies metamorphism are estimated at 750−830 °C and 18.5−21.2 kbar, followed by decompressional cooling through amphibolite-facies (690−740 °C and 8.6−12.6 kbar) to greenschist- and prehnite-pumpellyite−facies conditions. The Yadunling eclogites consist mainly of garnet + omphacite (completely symplectized in the matrix) + amphibole + quartz + (rutile + ilmenite) ± epidote ± calcite. They record a metamorphic history from prograde stage at 490−540 °C and 10.7−14.0 kbar to peak stage at 620−700 °C and 16.2−17.9 kbar, followed by near-isothermal decompression (620−670 °C and 8.7−12.7 kbar) and subsequent cooling to greenschist-facies conditions. Ion microprobe zircon U-Pb ages constrain eclogite protoliths to 359 ± 2 Ma (Gongmiao) and metamorphism to 310−295 Ma, with rutile cooling ages of 295−285 Ma. Trace element geochemical analyses indicate that the Gongmiao eclogites have an island arc geochemical signature, with bulk-rock initial εNd values of −1.0 to +2.7 and an average zircon initial εHf value of +11.1, whereas the Yadunling eclogites display affinities consistent with normal mid-ocean-ridge basalt, with initial εNd values of +5.5 to +6.2. These data suggest that their protoliths correspond to arc-type gabbros and oceanic basalts, respectively, formed in a backarc basin environment. Minor siliceous rocks (with terrigenous detritus) within or adjacent to the Yadunling eclogites likely experienced synchronous metamorphism and deformation along with the eclogites. However, the widespread gneissic granites and clastic metasedimentary rocks underwent only low-grade metamorphism at ca. 285 Ma. This implies that the eclogites were tectonically juxtaposed with these country rocks during their exhumation. The coeval Gongmiao, Yadunling, and Chaotanbi eclogites define a >100 km NE-SW−trending medium- to high-temperature eclogite belt on eastern Hainan Island. The formation of this eclogite belt is inferred to be related to the opening and closure of a backarc basin and subsequent arc-continent collision, which are induced by the retreating to advancing subduction of the western Paleo-Pacific oceanic lithosphere during the late Paleozoic.

item.title: Global geodynamics during the Archean: A geochemistry perspective on TTGs and mantle-derived rocks from several Cratons worldwide
item.dc:creator: Rodrigo S. Marimon
Chris J. Hawkesworth
Jaana Halla
Henrique Bruno
Monica Heilbron
item.description: Geodynamics in the early Archean has been the subject of intense debate and two predominant schools of thought have emerged. Uniformitarians argue that plate tectonics have dominated the Earth's history, albeit with some temporal differences. Conversely, non-uniformitarians support a distinct geodynamic mode operated in the early Archean, characterized by vertical motions and plume activity, with subordinate intermittent subduction. In this contribution, we use a worldwide database of tonalites-trondhjemites-granodiorites (TTGs) and mafic-ultramafic rocks, coupled with batch melting calculations, to constrain geodynamics in the Archean. TTGs are the dominant felsic igneous/metaigneous components of Archean cratons. Basaltic rocks (averages) were selected from well-constrained modern tectonic settings as sources for batch melting calculations to establish which might produce melts that are similar to Archean TTGs. Th/Nb was used to discriminate which TTGs, from a worldwide database, had source rocks similar to plateau (non-uniformitarian scenario) or island arc (uniformitarian scenario) basalts. Overall Th/Nb increases steadily with time and prior to ~3.2 Ga TTGs yielded lower Th/Nb values, consistent with mostly plateau non-subduction related basalts as sources. Similarly, Th/Nb of Archean mafic-ultramafic rocks increase with time and a predominance of low Th/Nb prior to ~3.0–3.3 Ga indicates a paucity of subduction-related setting, whereas higher Th/Nb after ~3.0–3.3 Ga point to widespread subduction. The data suggest that the Earth transitioned from a geodynamic mode dominated by vertical tectonics and plume activity with subordinate intermittent subduction at ~3.3–3.0 Ga to a regime akin to modern-style plate tectonics, dominated by magmatism in subduction zones. Published: 2026-06-06T23:05:35+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.608 (consider)

Relevance: 0.608 (consider)

Geodynamics in the early Archean has been the subject of intense debate and two predominant schools of thought have emerged. Uniformitarians argue that plate tectonics have dominated the Earth's history, albeit with some temporal differences. Conversely, non-uniformitarians support a distinct geodynamic mode operated in the early Archean, characterized by vertical motions and plume activity, with subordinate intermittent subduction. In this contribution, we use a worldwide database of tonalites-trondhjemites-granodiorites (TTGs) and mafic-ultramafic rocks, coupled with batch melting calculations, to constrain geodynamics in the Archean. TTGs are the dominant felsic igneous/metaigneous components of Archean cratons. Basaltic rocks (averages) were selected from well-constrained modern tectonic settings as sources for batch melting calculations to establish which might produce melts that are similar to Archean TTGs. Overall Th/Nb increases steadily with time and prior to ~3.2 Ga TTGs yielded lower Th/Nb values, consistent with mostly plateau non-subduction related basalts as sources. Similarly, Th/Nb of Archean mafic-ultramafic rocks increase with time and a predominance of low Th/Nb prior to ~3.0–3.3 Ga indicates a paucity of subduction-related setting, whereas higher Th/Nb after ~3.0–3.3 Ga point to widespread subduction. The data suggest that the Earth transitioned from a geodynamic mode dominated by vertical tectonics and plume activity with subordinate intermittent subduction at ~3.3–3.0 Ga to a regime akin to modern-style plate tectonics, dominated by magmatism in subduction zones.

item.title: Crustal architecture, accretionary evolution, and mineralization in the southern Central Asian orogenic belt (Solonker-Alxa region, Inner Mongolia) from the Paleozoic to early Mesozoic
item.dc:creator: Qiang Gao
Mao-Qiang Yan
Robert Moritz
Jan Marten Huizenga
Hong-Tao Zhu
Zhao-Tong Liu
Kun-Feng Qiu
Jun-Hao Wei
Jun Deng
item.description: Understanding continental crustal architecture and its control on mineral systems is fundamental to the earth sciences. Here we integrate zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock Nd-Hf isotopes, lithogeochemical data, and regional metallogenic records from Cambrian to Triassic magmatic rocks across the Solonker-Alxa region of Inner Mongolia to define crustal domains and their metallogenic significance. Two contrasting isotopic provinces are identified: (1) an enriched domain [εNd(t) < −4, εHf(t) < 0] encompassing the North China Craton−Alxa Block and (2) a depleted domain [εNd(t) > −4, εHf(t) > 0] typifies the Central Asian orogenic belt (CAOB). These domains are separated by the Chifeng-Bayan Obo fault, the area north of Chaganhua, and the Quagan Qulu-Tebai fault. Crustal thickness patterns show a belt of thinned crust (<40 km) along the Mandula, Solonker, Ganqimandu, Yingba, and northern Enger Us fault zones, marking the boundary between the Mongolia collage and the Tarim-North China collage. Variations in crustal thickness suggest that the Alxa Block is a distinct Precambrian terrane, separate from the North China Craton. The protracted subduction-accretion and slab rollback of the Paleo-Asian Ocean have driven significant crustal growth in the Solonker-Alxa region, with juvenile crust contributions estimated at 37% in the southern CAOB. Crustal maturation and felsic differentiation resulted from polyphase melting events involving mafic lower crust, oceanic crust, and the middle to upper crust. A systematic correlation exists between isotopic domains, crustal architecture, and metallogenic distribution. Depleted domains with thin crust (<50 km) preferentially host porphyry Cu (± Au), epithermal Pb-Zn-Cu, and volcanic massive sulphide-type Cu deposits, whereas enriched isotopic domains and their peripheries contain porphyry Mo, skarn Cu-Fe, vein-type Au deposits, and magmatic Cu-Ni sulfide deposits, indicating genetic links to ancient crustal recycling and metasomatized lithospheric mantle. This synthesis provides a coherent tectono-metallogenic framework for the Solonker-Alxa segment of the southern CAOB and offers guidance for mineral exploration. Published: 2026-06-09T19:58:54+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.606 (consider)

item.title: Fluid exsolution in porphyry-skarn magmas constrained from volatile-bearing minerals in the Late Triassic Saishitang complex, East Kunlun Orogen, China
item.dc:creator: Liang Pan
Jinyang Zhang
Yuanming Pan
Qin Huang
Qilin Wang
Jiaxing Chai
Lanxing Zhang
Zhenbing She
Changqian Ma
Jianwei Li
item.description: Fluid saturation and exsolution play critical roles in forming porphyry-skarn deposits, but the timing and depth of this process during transcrustal magmatic evolution are often difficult to determine. This study uses volatile compositions of amphibole, biotite, and apatite to reconstruct fluid exsolution during magmatic evolution in the Saishitang complex formed from a transcrustal magmatic system. The Saishitang complex is primarily associated with skarn Cu mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogen, western China and comprises a pluton consisting of quartz diorite, granodiorite, and monzogranite intruded by a series of porphyritic dykes of diorite, quartz diorite, granodiorite, and granite. Calculated F and Cl contents for corresponding melts from amphibole, biotite, and apatite of each rock type in the complex, as well as temperatures obtained from the amphibole geothermometer, reveal that an abrupt drop in Cl-H2O concentrations, marking the onset of fluid saturation at 780–900 ℃ in both porphyritic and equigranular rocks. The Al-in-amphibole geobarometer yields pressures of 81–180 MPa for fluid exsolution, corresponding to a paleodepth of 3.0–6.8 km. These results as well as fractional crystallization modeling show that the Saishitang magmatic system was Cl undersaturated at the middle to lower crustal levels before fluid exsolution in the upper crust. These conclusions are different from the recent model for fluid exsolution at the middle or lower crust suggested by melt inclusions. The study demonstrates that Cl and H2O trends can reliably trace fluid exsolution in the Saishitang complex and thus establishes a robust approach for reconstructing magmatic fluid saturation histories through the analysis of volatile-bearing minerals, particularly in settings where direct melt records are absent or compromised. item.content:encoded:
Authors: Liang Pan; Jinyang Zhang; Yuanming Pan; Qin Huang; Qilin Wang; Jiaxing Chai; Lanxing Zhang; Zhenbing She; Changqian Ma; Jianwei Li

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107367

Relevance: 0.602 (consider)

Fluid saturation and exsolution play critical roles in forming porphyry-skarn deposits, but the timing and depth of this process during transcrustal magmatic evolution are often difficult to determine. This study uses volatile compositions of amphibole, biotite, and apatite to reconstruct fluid exsolution during magmatic evolution in the Saishitang complex formed from a transcrustal magmatic system. The Saishitang complex is primarily associated with skarn Cu mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogen, western China and comprises a pluton consisting of quartz diorite, granodiorite, and monzogranite intruded by a series of porphyritic dykes of diorite, quartz diorite, granodiorite, and granite. Calculated F and Cl contents for corresponding melts from amphibole, biotite, and apatite of each rock type in the complex, as well as temperatures obtained from the amphibole geothermometer, reveal that an abrupt drop in Cl-H2O concentrations, marking the onset of fluid saturation at 780–900 ℃ in both porphyritic and equigranular rocks. The Al-in-amphibole geobarometer yields pressures of 81–180 MPa for fluid exsolution, corresponding to a paleodepth of 3.0–6.8 km. These results as well as fractional crystallization modeling show that the Saishitang magmatic system was Cl undersaturated at the middle to lower crustal levels before fluid exsolution in the upper crust. These conclusions are different from the recent model for fluid exsolution at the middle or lower crust suggested by melt inclusions. The study demonstrates that Cl and H2O trends can reliably trace fluid exsolution in the Saishitang complex and thus establishes a robust approach for reconstructing magmatic fluid saturation histories through the analysis of volatile-bearing minerals, particularly in settings where direct melt records are absent or compromised.

item.title: Geochemical and Isotopic Constraints on Long‐Lived Source Enrichment and Mantle Evolution in Paleoproterozoic Cratonic Lamprophyres
item.dc:creator: Sourav Naskar
Rohit Pandey
Nils Björn Baumann
N. V. Chalapathi Rao
George Mathew
J. Amal Dev
J. K. Tomson
item.description: Abstract The cratonic lithospheric mantle records complex metasomatic processes and is frequently tapped by alkaline magmatism, offering a unique opportunity to trace the progressive evolution of the mantle. In the present contribution, we investigate a newly identified calc‐alkaline lamprophyre field from the Neoarchean Jonnagiri Schist Belt, Eastern Dharwar Craton, southern India. Petrographic and mineralogical observations reveal reversely zoned amphibole phenocrysts, indicating magma mixing and recharge within a multi‐level magma plumbing system. Amphibole cores, interpreted as antecrysts, crystallized in a deep‐seated magma reservoir (∼20 km) and were later overgrown by rims equilibrated with a more mafic melt at shallower crustal levels (∼7 km), recording a dynamic history of magmatic evolution. 40 Ar/ 39 Ar age (2,200–2,400 Ma) of the lamprophyres cast a significantly distinct Paleoproterozoic calc‐alkaline event that predates the widespread Mesoproterozoic (∼1,100 Ma) alkaline magmatism elsewhere in the craton. Whole‐rock geochemistry characterized by negative Nb‐Ta, Zr‐Hf, and Ti anomalies in the spidergram, suggests derivation from a sub‐continental lithospheric mantle source modified by slab‐derived fluids during the Neoarchean subduction. The Th/Yb (5.3–2.5) and Nb/U (9.4–2.1) trace‐element ratios, and the fractionated LREE/HREE pattern (La N /Yb N : 29–18) of the investigated lamprophyres are indistinguishable from those of lamprophyres from the Superior‐ and Yilgarn‐ Cratons. Near Chondritic εNd i signatures and enriched Archean mantle‐like Sr isotopic signatures imply a short residence time of the generated magma after metasomatic modification. The geodynamic setup of the Jonnagiri schist belt reflects post‐collisional reworking of the subduction‐modified lithosphere, possibly triggered by the slab break‐off and asthenospheric upwelling during the waning period of Neoarchean accretion. Partial melting of this enriched mantle during the Paleoproterozoic (∼2.3 Ga), likely related to Superia supercontinent break‐up, contributed to the polychronous magmatic activity in this domain. The Jonnagiri lamprophyres record the prolonged influence of a subduction‐enriched lithospheric domain tapped episodically during the Neoarchean to Mesoproterozoic. Published: 2026-06-05T14:55:14+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.599 (consider)

Authors: Sourav Naskar; Rohit Pandey; Nils Björn Baumann; N. V. Chalapathi Rao; George Mathew; J. Amal Dev; J. K. Tomson

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012773

Relevance: 0.599 (consider)

Abstract The cratonic lithospheric mantle records complex metasomatic processes and is frequently tapped by alkaline magmatism, offering a unique opportunity to trace the progressive evolution of the mantle. In the present contribution, we investigate a newly identified calc‐alkaline lamprophyre field from the Neoarchean Jonnagiri Schist Belt, Eastern Dharwar Craton, southern India. Petrographic and mineralogical observations reveal reversely zoned amphibole phenocrysts, indicating magma mixing and recharge within a multi‐level magma plumbing system. Amphibole cores, interpreted as antecrysts, crystallized in a deep‐seated magma reservoir (∼20 km) and were later overgrown by rims equilibrated with a more mafic melt at shallower crustal levels (∼7 km), recording a dynamic history of magmatic evolution. 40 Ar/ 39 Ar age (2,200–2,400 Ma) of the lamprophyres cast a significantly distinct Paleoproterozoic calc‐alkaline event that predates the widespread Mesoproterozoic (∼1,100 Ma) alkaline magmatism elsewhere in the craton. Whole‐rock geochemistry characterized by negative Nb‐Ta, Zr‐Hf, and Ti anomalies in the spidergram, suggests derivation from a sub‐continental lithospheric mantle source modified by slab‐derived fluids during the Neoarchean subduction. The Th/Yb (5.3–2.5) and Nb/U (9.4–2.1) trace‐element ratios, and the fractionated LREE/HREE pattern (La N /Yb N : 29–18) of the investigated lamprophyres are indistinguishable from those of lamprophyres from the Superior‐ and Yilgarn‐ Cratons. Near Chondritic εNd i signatures and enriched Archean mantle‐like Sr isotopic signatures imply a short residence time of the generated magma after metasomatic modification. The geodynamic setup of the Jonnagiri schist belt reflects post‐collisional reworking of the subduction‐modified lithosphere, possibly triggered by the slab break‐off and asthenospheric upwelling during the waning period of Neoarchean accretion. Partial melting of this enriched mantle during the Paleoproterozoic (∼2.3 Ga), likely related to Superia supercontinent break‐up, contributed to the polychronous magmatic activity in this domain. The Jonnagiri lamprophyres record the prolonged influence of a subduction‐enriched lithospheric domain tapped episodically during the Neoarchean to Mesoproterozoic.

item.title: Rare earth element enrichment through multi-stage magmatic differentiation and tectono-metamorphic processes: an example from the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, central Korean Peninsula
item.dc:creator: Vinod O. Samuel
Yirang Jang
Sanghoon Kwon
Sang-Tae Kim
Sungshil Kim
item.description: This study investigates igneous and metamorphic pathways of Rare Earth Elements (REE) enrichment in the Hongcheon carbonatite complex in the central part of the Korean Peninsula and its tectonic implications. This Fe- and P-rich carbonatite complex hosts an estimated 382,600 tons monazite ore reserve and is compositionally analogous to the world’s largest Mesoproterozoic Bayan Obo carbonatite complex in China. The Hongcheon carbonatite complex comprises four main units: a dolomite carbonatite (DC), a magnetite segment (MS), a phosphate segment (PS), and an aureole of Na-rich fenite. The DC unit shows an orthocumulate texture, composed of medium-grained dolomite with minor calcite, suggesting early stages of carbonatite melt differentiation. Whereas subsequent stages formed magnetite and phosphate-rich melt segments, the Na-rich fenite aureole indicates the expulsion of alkali-rich, especially Na-rich, melt/fluid from the carbonatite melt during crystallization. Our geochemical analyses indicate that interaction between the alkali-rich melt/fluid and the country rock does not result in significant REE concentration. Within the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, the PS hosts REE-rich minerals, such as apatite and monazite, whereas apatite and monazite are largely absent from the DC and MS units except where PS occurs. Textural relationships among minerals in the PS suggest that apatite might have preferentially crystallized in Ca- and P-rich melt. Extensive monazite mineralization occurred through apatite dissolution by fluid-assisted recrystallization under amphibolite facies metasomatic conditions, with subsequent calcite and quartz precipitation in the matrix and fracture-filling veins. These results and published (Permo-)Triassic monazite age data suggest that extensive monazite formation was likely influenced by tectono-metamorphic processes, reflecting later metamorphic overprinting shortly after peak (Permo-)Triassic collisional orogeny in the central Korean Peninsula. Overall, this study highlights that Ca-phosphate melts may have an important role in extracting REEs from carbonatite melt, whereas reactive fluids associated with later tectono-metamorphic events likely influence the redistribution and final mineralogical composition of REE ores (e.g., phosphate, fluoride, etc.) in large carbonatite complexes. Published: 2026-06-10T15:25:50+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.596 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107372

Relevance: 0.596 (consider)

This study investigates igneous and metamorphic pathways of Rare Earth Elements (REE) enrichment in the Hongcheon carbonatite complex in the central part of the Korean Peninsula and its tectonic implications. This Fe- and P-rich carbonatite complex hosts an estimated 382,600 tons monazite ore reserve and is compositionally analogous to the world’s largest Mesoproterozoic Bayan Obo carbonatite complex in China. The Hongcheon carbonatite complex comprises four main units: a dolomite carbonatite (DC), a magnetite segment (MS), a phosphate segment (PS), and an aureole of Na-rich fenite. The DC unit shows an orthocumulate texture, composed of medium-grained dolomite with minor calcite, suggesting early stages of carbonatite melt differentiation. Whereas subsequent stages formed magnetite and phosphate-rich melt segments, the Na-rich fenite aureole indicates the expulsion of alkali-rich, especially Na-rich, melt/fluid from the carbonatite melt during crystallization. Our geochemical analyses indicate that interaction between the alkali-rich melt/fluid and the country rock does not result in significant REE concentration. Within the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, the PS hosts REE-rich minerals, such as apatite and monazite, whereas apatite and monazite are largely absent from the DC and MS units except where PS occurs. Textural relationships among minerals in the PS suggest that apatite might have preferentially crystallized in Ca- and P-rich melt. Extensive monazite mineralization occurred through apatite dissolution by fluid-assisted recrystallization under amphibolite facies metasomatic conditions, with subsequent calcite and quartz precipitation in the matrix and fracture-filling veins. These results and published (Permo-)Triassic monazite age data suggest that extensive monazite formation was likely influenced by tectono-metamorphic processes, reflecting later metamorphic overprinting shortly after peak (Permo-)Triassic collisional orogeny in the central Korean Peninsula. Overall, this study highlights that Ca-phosphate melts may have an important role in extracting REEs from carbonatite melt, whereas reactive fluids associated with later tectono-metamorphic events likely influence the redistribution and final mineralogical composition of REE ores (e.g., phosphate, fluoride, etc.) in large carbonatite complexes.

item.title: Termination of the Trans-North China Orogenesis: Evidence for ∼1.81 Ga Rapid Cooling and Exhumation in the Zanhuang Complex
item.dc:creator: Wen Zhang
Pinghua Liu
Lei Zou
Lilin Du
Chonghui Yang
Hua Xiang
Guangyu Huang
Wanpeng Zhou
Jianlong Wang
Qianyu Li
Hao Sun
Guochun Zhao
item.description: The Trans-North China Orogen (TNCO), marking the final amalgamation of the Western and Eastern Blocks of the North China Craton (NCC), provides key insights into Paleoproterozoic crustal evolution and the assembly of the Columbia supercontinent. Here, we investigate garnet-bearing amphibolites derived from mafic volcanic protoliths of the Liyanghe Formation (Zanhuang Group) in the Zanhuang Complex of the central TNCO. Petrography and phase equilibria modeling delineate a clockwise P–T trajectory suggesting a peak high-pressure (HP) amphibolite-facies stage (M2: 1.32–1.63 GPa, 715–750 °C) followed by near-isothermal decompression to lower-pressure amphibolite-facies conditions (M3: 0.61–0.88 GPa, 680–718 °C). Furthermore, high-Ti amphibole compositions and oriented rutile exsolution within garnet provide evidence for a cryptic granulite-facies thermal peak, the diagnostic mineral records of which were likely obliterated by pervasive suprasolidus re-equilibration. Multi-mineral LA-ICP-MS U–Pb geochronology reveals a probable temporal decoupling between peak and cooling stages: metamorphic zircon dating constrains the timing of peak metamorphism to 1870 ± 8 Ma and 1868 ± 8 Ma, whereas cooling phases are consistently younger, with titanite at 1810 ± 16 Ma, rutile at 1799 ± 12 Ma and 1805 ± 6 Ma, and apatite at 1812 ± 31 Ma. Our results demonstrate a prolonged deep crustal residence (∼60 Myr) with negligible cooling (<1 °C/Myr), followed by rapid exhumation at ∼1.81 Ga with a cooling rate of 11.8 - 5.8 + 23.9 °C/Myr. These findings support a Paleoproterozoic final collision model for the TNCO and attribute this rapid ∼1.81 Ga exhumation to lithospheric delamination or slab break-off, marking the definitive collapse of the orogen. Published: 2026-06-05T16:48:37+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.591 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wen Zhang; Pinghua Liu; Lei Zou; Lilin Du; Chonghui Yang; Hua Xiang; Guangyu Huang; Wanpeng Zhou; Jianlong Wang; Qianyu Li; Hao Sun; Guochun Zhao

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108162

Relevance: 0.591 (consider)

The Trans-North China Orogen (TNCO), marking the final amalgamation of the Western and Eastern Blocks of the North China Craton (NCC), provides key insights into Paleoproterozoic crustal evolution and the assembly of the Columbia supercontinent. Here, we investigate garnet-bearing amphibolites derived from mafic volcanic protoliths of the Liyanghe Formation (Zanhuang Group) in the Zanhuang Complex of the central TNCO. Petrography and phase equilibria modeling delineate a clockwise P–T trajectory suggesting a peak high-pressure (HP) amphibolite-facies stage (M2: 1.32–1.63 GPa, 715–750 °C) followed by near-isothermal decompression to lower-pressure amphibolite-facies conditions (M3: 0.61–0.88 GPa, 680–718 °C). Furthermore, high-Ti amphibole compositions and oriented rutile exsolution within garnet provide evidence for a cryptic granulite-facies thermal peak, the diagnostic mineral records of which were likely obliterated by pervasive suprasolidus re-equilibration. Multi-mineral LA-ICP-MS U–Pb geochronology reveals a probable temporal decoupling between peak and cooling stages: metamorphic zircon dating constrains the timing of peak metamorphism to 1870 ± 8 Ma and 1868 ± 8 Ma, whereas cooling phases are consistently younger, with titanite at 1810 ± 16 Ma, rutile at 1799 ± 12 Ma and 1805 ± 6 Ma, and apatite at 1812 ± 31 Ma. Our results demonstrate a prolonged deep crustal residence (∼60 Myr) with negligible cooling (<1 °C/Myr), followed by rapid exhumation at ∼1.81 Ga with a cooling rate of 11.8 - 5.8 + 23.9 °C/Myr. These findings support a Paleoproterozoic final collision model for the TNCO and attribute this rapid ∼1.81 Ga exhumation to lithospheric delamination or slab break-off, marking the definitive collapse of the orogen.

item.title: Mineralization characteristics and controlling factors of key metals of Re, Se, and Te in the Bainaimiao Cu-Au-Mo deposit, northern margin of North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Zhenhua Zhou
Renyu Liang
Qing Zhang
Cheng Zhang
Hongwei Yuan
item.description: Porphyry copper deposits are the most important source of critical minerals such as Re, Se, and Te. The Bainaimiao Cu-Au-Mo deposit is located in the eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). The northern ore section exhibits porphyry-type alteration and mineralization characteristics, while the ore bodies in the southern ore section mainly occur as stratiform-like layers within the country-rock greenschist. There has long been debate regarding the genetic type of the Bainaimiao deposit. Through in-situ component analysis of different types of sulfides such as pyrite and chalcopyrite, combining with previous data and literatures, we propose that the Bainaimiao belongs to a porphyry deposit modified by later orogenic processes. The high Re content in molybdenite from the Bainaimiao deposit (109–1051 ppm, average of 627 ppm) may be controlled by factors likely its relatively low oxygen fugacity and the incorporation of terrigenous sedimentary components in the magma source. Chalcopyrite from different ore sections have high Se contents, averaging ca. 100 ppm, indicating that chalcopyrite is the primary host mineral for Se. The enrichment of Se is mainly achieved through coupled substitution between S2⁻ and Se2⁻. Tellurium concentrations are relatively low, primarily entering the chalcopyrite lattice as bismuth-tellurium mineral inclusions, which may be related to crustal contamination or the lack of competitive sulfides during Cu mineralization. Porphyry copper deposits in the CAOB have significant potential for mineralization of dispersed elements such as Re, Se, and Te. Future efforts should focus on study of mineralization processes and the comprehensive utilization and recovery of these key metal elements. Published: 2026-06-08T16:26:46+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.591 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107370

Porphyry copper deposits are the most important source of critical minerals such as Re, Se, and Te. The Bainaimiao Cu-Au-Mo deposit is located in the eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). The northern ore section exhibits porphyry-type alteration and mineralization characteristics, while the ore bodies in the southern ore section mainly occur as stratiform-like layers within the country-rock greenschist. There has long been debate regarding the genetic type of the Bainaimiao deposit. Through in-situ component analysis of different types of sulfides such as pyrite and chalcopyrite, combining with previous data and literatures, we propose that the Bainaimiao belongs to a porphyry deposit modified by later orogenic processes. The high Re content in molybdenite from the Bainaimiao deposit (109–1051 ppm, average of 627 ppm) may be controlled by factors likely its relatively low oxygen fugacity and the incorporation of terrigenous sedimentary components in the magma source. Chalcopyrite from different ore sections have high Se contents, averaging ca. 100 ppm, indicating that chalcopyrite is the primary host mineral for Se. The enrichment of Se is mainly achieved through coupled substitution between S2⁻ and Se2⁻. Tellurium concentrations are relatively low, primarily entering the chalcopyrite lattice as bismuth-tellurium mineral inclusions, which may be related to crustal contamination or the lack of competitive sulfides during Cu mineralization. Porphyry copper deposits in the CAOB have significant potential for mineralization of dispersed elements such as Re, Se, and Te. Future efforts should focus on study of mineralization processes and the comprehensive utilization and recovery of these key metal elements.

item.title: Pre-main-ore fluid pulses and fluid pathway preparation for Mo enrichment in the Rongga porphyry Mo deposit, Tibet, China
item.dc:creator: Jie Zhang
Bo Peng
Fengqin Ran
Liqiang Wang
Bin Liu
Baolong Li
Kelin Huang
item.description: Porphyry Mo deposits commonly form through multistage magmatic–hydrothermal processes, but the role of pre-main-ore fluid pulses in Mo pre-enrichment and fluid pathway preparation remains poorly constrained. The Rongga deposit is the first recognized porphyry Mo system in the otherwise Cu–Au-dominated Bangong–Nujiang metallogenic belt, Tibet. Its ore-related magmas were derived from a relatively Mo-poor juvenile lower-crust. Here, we combine refined quartz-vein classification with pyrite LA-ICP-MS spot analyses, elemental mapping and unsupervised clustering to evaluate mineral-scale records of fluid influx and Mo enrichment. The vein system includes early A quartz–pyrite veins, transitional AB quartz–pyrite–molybdenite veins, main-stage B quartz–molybdenite veins and late D quartz–pyrite ± polymetallic sulfide veins. Pyrite trace-element data show a stage-related shift from Co–Ni–As-rich A-vein pyrite to mixed Co–Ni–As and Cu–Ag–Zn–Mn–Mo signatures in AB-vein pyrite, Cu–Ag–Zn–Mn–Mo-enriched B-vein pyrite and Pb–Bi–Te-rich D-vein pyrite. AB-vein pyrite occupies an intermediate but distinguishable position between A- and B-vein pyrite. Mo statistics show that B-vein pyrite has the highest median Mo content, whereas AB-vein pyrite records the highest maximum and mean Mo contents. Elemental maps and time-resolved signals further show that Mo-rich domains in AB-vein pyrite occur in both internal and rim/fracture-related domains and reflect mixed Mo-bearing components, including submicroscopic phases and fracture-related fillings, rather than purely lattice-bound Mo in pyrite. These results indicate that Mo enrichment at Rongga was not restricted to the main B-vein stage. We suggest that episodic AB-stage fluid influx promoted localized Mo pre-enrichment and reactivation of earlier vein structures, thereby preparing favorable sites for focused molybdenite precipitation during B-vein formation. Published: 2026-06-06T03:54:11+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.590 (consider)

Porphyry Mo deposits commonly form through multistage magmatic–hydrothermal processes, but the role of pre-main-ore fluid pulses in Mo pre-enrichment and fluid pathway preparation remains poorly constrained. The Rongga deposit is the first recognized porphyry Mo system in the otherwise Cu–Au-dominated Bangong–Nujiang metallogenic belt, Tibet. Pyrite trace-element data show a stage-related shift from Co–Ni–As-rich A-vein pyrite to mixed Co–Ni–As and Cu–Ag–Zn–Mn–Mo signatures in AB-vein pyrite, Cu–Ag–Zn–Mn–Mo-enriched B-vein pyrite and Pb–Bi–Te-rich D-vein pyrite. Elemental maps and time-resolved signals further show that Mo-rich domains in AB-vein pyrite occur in both internal and rim/fracture-related domains and reflect mixed Mo-bearing components, including submicroscopic phases and fracture-related fillings, rather than purely lattice-bound Mo in pyrite. These results indicate that Mo enrichment at Rongga was not restricted to the main B-vein stage. We suggest that episodic AB-stage fluid influx promoted localized Mo pre-enrichment and reactivation of earlier vein structures, thereby preparing favorable sites for focused molybdenite precipitation during B-vein formation.

item.title: Late Triassic–Early Jurassic I-type leucogranites and quartz diorites from the Kaçkar Batholith (Eastern Pontides, NE Türkiye): Implications for the generation of highly evolved felsic magmas at convergent plate boundaries
item.dc:creator: Yener Eyuboglu
item.description: This study examines Late Triassic–Early Jurassic I-type leucogranites and quartz diorites from the Kaçkar Batholith (NE Türkiye) to constrain the origin of highly evolved felsic magmas at convergent plate boundaries. Zircon UPb ages indicate crystallization of the Yedigöl quartz diorites and Sırakonaklar leucogranites at ~210–207 Ma, whereas the Altıparmak leucogranites record a younger felsic event at ~192 Ma, documenting episodic magmatism across the Triassic–Jurassic transition. The quartz diorites are calc-alkaline and metaluminous, displaying positive zircon Ce anomalies without Eu depletion, consistent with oxidized, hydrous, mantle-influenced magmas and limited plagioclase fractionation. In contrast, the leucogranites are evolved, weakly peraluminous, and characterized by very low Sr contents and pronounced negative Eu anomalies in both whole-rock and zircon chemistry, reflecting strong feldspar-controlled internal differentiation. The leucogranites include perthite- and antiperthite-bearing varieties, recording contrasting KNa feldspar systematics during magma evolution. All lithologies display mantle-influenced Sr–Nd–Pb and zircon LuHf isotopic signatures consistent with an I-type, juvenile-dominated origin, excluding derivation from metasedimentary sources. Geochemical constraints indicate that the leucogranites did not form through simple fractional crystallization of quartz diorite magmas, but instead originated from feldspar-retentive partial melting of meta-igneous lower crust, driven by sustained thermal input from mantle-influenced magmatism within a vertically coupled arc system and followed by feldspar-dominated differentiation. Enrichment in Nb, Ta, Th, and U reflects magmatic fertility and late-stage accessory mineral concentration rather than economically significant rare-metal mineralization. Overall, the results highlight episodic felsic magma generation above a south-dipping subduction zone during Late Triassic–Early Jurassic. Published: 2026-06-05T15:37:35+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.589 (consider)

Authors: Yener Eyuboglu

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108654

Relevance: 0.589 (consider)

This study examines Late Triassic–Early Jurassic I-type leucogranites and quartz diorites from the Kaçkar Batholith (NE Türkiye) to constrain the origin of highly evolved felsic magmas at convergent plate boundaries. Zircon UPb ages indicate crystallization of the Yedigöl quartz diorites and Sırakonaklar leucogranites at ~210–207 Ma, whereas the Altıparmak leucogranites record a younger felsic event at ~192 Ma, documenting episodic magmatism across the Triassic–Jurassic transition. The quartz diorites are calc-alkaline and metaluminous, displaying positive zircon Ce anomalies without Eu depletion, consistent with oxidized, hydrous, mantle-influenced magmas and limited plagioclase fractionation. In contrast, the leucogranites are evolved, weakly peraluminous, and characterized by very low Sr contents and pronounced negative Eu anomalies in both whole-rock and zircon chemistry, reflecting strong feldspar-controlled internal differentiation. The leucogranites include perthite- and antiperthite-bearing varieties, recording contrasting KNa feldspar systematics during magma evolution. All lithologies display mantle-influenced Sr–Nd–Pb and zircon LuHf isotopic signatures consistent with an I-type, juvenile-dominated origin, excluding derivation from metasedimentary sources. Geochemical constraints indicate that the leucogranites did not form through simple fractional crystallization of quartz diorite magmas, but instead originated from feldspar-retentive partial melting of meta-igneous lower crust, driven by sustained thermal input from mantle-influenced magmatism within a vertically coupled arc system and followed by feldspar-dominated differentiation. Enrichment in Nb, Ta, Th, and U reflects magmatic fertility and late-stage accessory mineral concentration rather than economically significant rare-metal mineralization. Overall, the results highlight episodic felsic magma generation above a south-dipping subduction zone during Late Triassic–Early Jurassic.

item.title: Review on the genesis of the world-class Imiter Ag-Hg deposit: evidence of post-collisional delamination-related metal sources in the Northwestern edge of the West African Craton
item.dc:creator: Mehdi Ousbih
Moha Ikenne
Brian Cousens
Yves Geraud
Fatiha Askkour
Alexiane Favier
Hafida El Bilali
Oubaassine Hassane
Mohamed Ez-Zghoudy
Abdelaziz Gaouzi
Richard Ernst
item.description: The Imiter Ag District, situated in Morocco’s Anti-Atlas belt in the northwestern edge of the West African Craton, hosts silver-rich base metal mineralization across several sectors, including Imiter I, Imiter II, Imiter South, and Igoudrane. The mineralization is characterized by sub-horizontal ore bodies, steeply dipping north-trending veins aligned with major faults, and low-angle south-dipping veins associated with large-scale fault zones. A key factor in the formation of this world-class and extensive mineralization is the persistent and robust magmatic source, coupled with significant tectonic structures linked to asthenospheric mantle reservoirs. This study combines field observations, electron probe microanalysis, and isotopic data with a review of previously published geochemical and U-Pb zircon ages from magmatic rocks to trace the origin of lead in silver-associated galena. Pb isotopic data reveal three distinct lead signatures corresponding to three geological episodes: Pb1, associated with Late Ediacaran magmatism; Pb2, linked to Hercynian orogenic hydrothermal processes; and Pb3, attributed to Alpine magmatism. The primary mineralization (Pb1) is related to Late Ediacaran high Sr/Y intrusions at Bou Fliou (582 Ma) and Igoudrane (538 Ma), as well as dykes controlled by the crustal-scale Imiter Fault. Precious metal remobilization (Au, Ag) is associated with magma derived from the delaminated lower crust and a former subducted slab during post-collisional processes, with significant mantle-derived magmatic input. Dykes play a role in mineralization by acting as conduits for mineralizing fluids. The formation of magma-related mineral deposits in the Imiter region is attributed to a regional metallogenic event during the Late Ediacaran Large Igneous Province (590–540 Ma), a period of extensive magmatism that was critical for the development of intrusions-related mineral deposits in the Anti-Atlas belt. Published: 2026-06-09T15:49:06+00:00 Venue: Gondwana Research Score: 0.589 (consider)

Authors: Mehdi Ousbih; Moha Ikenne; Brian Cousens; Yves Geraud; Fatiha Askkour; Alexiane Favier; Hafida El Bilali; Oubaassine Hassane; Mohamed Ez-Zghoudy; Abdelaziz Gaouzi; Richard Ernst

Journal: Gondwana Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.gr.2026.05.007

Relevance: 0.589 (consider)

The Imiter Ag District, situated in Morocco’s Anti-Atlas belt in the northwestern edge of the West African Craton, hosts silver-rich base metal mineralization across several sectors, including Imiter I, Imiter II, Imiter South, and Igoudrane. The mineralization is characterized by sub-horizontal ore bodies, steeply dipping north-trending veins aligned with major faults, and low-angle south-dipping veins associated with large-scale fault zones. A key factor in the formation of this world-class and extensive mineralization is the persistent and robust magmatic source, coupled with significant tectonic structures linked to asthenospheric mantle reservoirs. This study combines field observations, electron probe microanalysis, and isotopic data with a review of previously published geochemical and U-Pb zircon ages from magmatic rocks to trace the origin of lead in silver-associated galena. Pb isotopic data reveal three distinct lead signatures corresponding to three geological episodes: Pb1, associated with Late Ediacaran magmatism; Pb2, linked to Hercynian orogenic hydrothermal processes; and Pb3, attributed to Alpine magmatism. The primary mineralization (Pb1) is related to Late Ediacaran high Sr/Y intrusions at Bou Fliou (582 Ma) and Igoudrane (538 Ma), as well as dykes controlled by the crustal-scale Imiter Fault. Precious metal remobilization (Au, Ag) is associated with magma derived from the delaminated lower crust and a former subducted slab during post-collisional processes, with significant mantle-derived magmatic input. Dykes play a role in mineralization by acting as conduits for mineralizing fluids. The formation of magma-related mineral deposits in the Imiter region is attributed to a regional metallogenic event during the Late Ediacaran Large Igneous Province (590–540 Ma), a period of extensive magmatism that was critical for the development of intrusions-related mineral deposits in the Anti-Atlas belt.

item.title: When tholeiitic magma mimic alkaline magma: Formation of Na-Ca amphiboles and aenigmatite driven by fluid contamination
item.dc:creator: Alban Cheviet
Christophe Galerne
Martine Buatier
Wolfgang Bach
Anette Meixner
Flavien Choulet
item.description: The emplacement of tholeiitic sills into unconsolidated, volatile-rich sediments provides a unique context to investigate magma–fluid–sediment interactions and their effects on crystallisation. We present petrographic, geochemical, isotopic, and thermodynamic evidence from the ~75 m thick sill at Site U1546 of IODP Expedition 385 in the Guaymas Basin. The sill displays a heterogeneous internal architecture composed of basaltic margins enriched in K₂O and biotite, a doleritic core representative of the primary tholeiitic magma, and coarse-grained gabbroic domains enriched in Na₂O and containing amphibole and aenigmatite. The rocks that compose the pseudogabbroic domains display a texture similar to that gabbro, but their origin is unclear. For this reason, the term “pseudogabbro” will be used throughout the text to refer to them. Geochemical and isotopic data indicate that all lithologies originate from the same parental magma, but their mineralogical differences reflect local contamination and hydration processes. The basaltic margins are contaminated by the infiltration of K-rich metamorphic fluids generated in the contact aureoles, whereas the pseudogabbros formed through fractional crystallisation of a hydrated residual melt of highly alkaline composition, with amphibole and aenigmatite stabilised under variable redox conditions. Thermodynamic models support a two-step crystallisation history involving (1) equilibrium crystallisation of the primary magma, followed by (2) hydration of an incipient mush by infiltration of sediment pore waters, accompanied by fluctuations in fO₂ and late fluid exsolution. These processes illustrate how tholeiitic magmas emplaced in sedimentary basins can mimic alkaline systems through fluid-driven contamination, resulting in the formation of mineralogical assemblages atypical of tholeiitic intrusions. Such mechanisms are critical for understanding volatile transfer, mineral diversity, and redox evolution in shallow magmatic systems. Published: 2026-06-06T03:51:08+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.588 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Alban Cheviet; Christophe Galerne; Martine Buatier; Wolfgang Bach; Anette Meixner; Flavien Choulet

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108662

Relevance: 0.588 (consider)

The emplacement of tholeiitic sills into unconsolidated, volatile-rich sediments provides a unique context to investigate magma–fluid–sediment interactions and their effects on crystallisation. We present petrographic, geochemical, isotopic, and thermodynamic evidence from the ~75 m thick sill at Site U1546 of IODP Expedition 385 in the Guaymas Basin. The sill displays a heterogeneous internal architecture composed of basaltic margins enriched in K₂O and biotite, a doleritic core representative of the primary tholeiitic magma, and coarse-grained gabbroic domains enriched in Na₂O and containing amphibole and aenigmatite. The rocks that compose the pseudogabbroic domains display a texture similar to that gabbro, but their origin is unclear. For this reason, the term “pseudogabbro” will be used throughout the text to refer to them. Geochemical and isotopic data indicate that all lithologies originate from the same parental magma, but their mineralogical differences reflect local contamination and hydration processes. The basaltic margins are contaminated by the infiltration of K-rich metamorphic fluids generated in the contact aureoles, whereas the pseudogabbros formed through fractional crystallisation of a hydrated residual melt of highly alkaline composition, with amphibole and aenigmatite stabilised under variable redox conditions. Thermodynamic models support a two-step crystallisation history involving (1) equilibrium crystallisation of the primary magma, followed by (2) hydration of an incipient mush by infiltration of sediment pore waters, accompanied by fluctuations in fO₂ and late fluid exsolution. These processes illustrate how tholeiitic magmas emplaced in sedimentary basins can mimic alkaline systems through fluid-driven contamination, resulting in the formation of mineralogical assemblages atypical of tholeiitic intrusions. Such mechanisms are critical for understanding volatile transfer, mineral diversity, and redox evolution in shallow magmatic systems.

item.title: Heavy silicon isotope and mass-independent mercury isotope fractionation signals of Archean TTGs: Insights into the recycling of the silicified oceanic crust in the source region
item.dc:creator: Xiao Hu
Wenbin Zhu
Haizhen Wei
Runsheng Yin
Yongxiang Li
Yiwei Rong
Di Chen
item.description: Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG), the main constituent of the Archean Earth's continental crust, is widely believed to originate from the partial melting of hydrated metamorphic basalts. However, the tectonic setting of the TTG's origin and thus the Archean Earth evolution remains poorly understood. In this study, we performed silicon (Si) isotope, mercury (Hg) isotope, and geochemical data analysis on 3.06 Ga to 2.5 Ga TTG samples from the Dunhuang Block, at the eastern margin of the Tarim Craton, to investigate their protolith composition and the formation environment. The δ30Si values of quartz in these Dunhuang TTG samples range from +0.05 ± 0.07‰ (2SD) to +0.25 ± 0.08‰ (2SD). These consistently heavy quartz Si isotope compositions may indicate that the silicified oceanic crust was incorporated into their protoliths. The Δ199Hg values of the 3.06–2.7 Ga Dunhuang TTG samples range from −0.05‰ to +0.09‰. In contrast, the 2.6–2.5 Ga samples exhibit a wider range (−0.11‰ to +0.14‰), indicating that the younger Dunhuang TTG protolith composition were no longer homogeneous and incorporated supracrustal materials. Coupled SiHg isotopes suggest that supracrustal recycling was a key process in the generation of the Dunhuang TTGs, and that the protolith composition of the 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs experienced a change compared to the earlier Dunhuang TTG samples. Combined with phase equilibrium modeling and geochemical data, we speculate that the subduction model may represent a reasonable explanation for the formation environment of 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs, although non-subduction models cannot be ruled out. Published: 2026-06-05T15:36:05+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.588 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiao Hu; Wenbin Zhu; Haizhen Wei; Runsheng Yin; Yongxiang Li; Yiwei Rong; Di Chen

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123558

Relevance: 0.588 (consider)

Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG), the main constituent of the Archean Earth's continental crust, is widely believed to originate from the partial melting of hydrated metamorphic basalts. However, the tectonic setting of the TTG's origin and thus the Archean Earth evolution remains poorly understood. In this study, we performed silicon (Si) isotope, mercury (Hg) isotope, and geochemical data analysis on 3.06 Ga to 2.5 Ga TTG samples from the Dunhuang Block, at the eastern margin of the Tarim Craton, to investigate their protolith composition and the formation environment. The δ30Si values of quartz in these Dunhuang TTG samples range from +0.05 ± 0.07‰ (2SD) to +0.25 ± 0.08‰ (2SD). These consistently heavy quartz Si isotope compositions may indicate that the silicified oceanic crust was incorporated into their protoliths. The Δ199Hg values of the 3.06–2.7 Ga Dunhuang TTG samples range from −0.05‰ to +0.09‰. In contrast, the 2.6–2.5 Ga samples exhibit a wider range (−0.11‰ to +0.14‰), indicating that the younger Dunhuang TTG protolith composition were no longer homogeneous and incorporated supracrustal materials. Coupled SiHg isotopes suggest that supracrustal recycling was a key process in the generation of the Dunhuang TTGs, and that the protolith composition of the 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs experienced a change compared to the earlier Dunhuang TTG samples. Combined with phase equilibrium modeling and geochemical data, we speculate that the subduction model may represent a reasonable explanation for the formation environment of 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs, although non-subduction models cannot be ruled out.

item.title: Spatial-temporal distribution of late Cenozoic intraplate basalts in the Leiqiong area, South China: Insights into the distinct roles of mantle plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement
item.dc:creator: Guo-Hong Sun
Xiao-Long Huang
Fan Yang
Yang Yu
Sheng-Zhu Zhu
item.description: The emplacement of plume head is a critical aspect of mantle plume theory; however, the respective roles of the plume and lithosphere during the emplacement remain insufficiently understood. The late Cenozoic Hainan mantle plume, inferred to remain in the plume head stage, preserves records of its emplacement processes. This study presents comprehensive geochemical data, including major and trace elements, as well as Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopes, for late Cenozoic basalts from the northern Leiqiong area (including Leizhou Peninsula, northern Hainan Island, and Weizhou Island), which are associated with the Hainan plume. Based on their spatial-temporal distribution, these basalts are categorized into early and late stages. Early-stage basalts, restricted to eastern Leiqiong area, show positive ΔεHf values (0.51–2.34) and consistently low Ce/Pb (7.22–18.24) and Nb/Th (6.78–10.52) ratios, suggesting a significant contribution from recycled sedimentary components. Late-stage basalts, distributed across the northern Leiqiong area, are subdivided into two groups based on their ΔεHf values. The eastern late-stage basalts display positive ΔεHf values with overall low Ce/Pb and Nb/Th ratios, whereas the western late-stage basalts show negative ΔεHf values (−1.71 to −0.01) with overall higher Ce/Pb (14.59–31.78) and Nb/Th (10.64–12.77) ratios, indicating a substantial contribution of recycled oceanic crust in their mantle source. The spatial-temporal distribution and geochemical variations of the Leiqiong basalts highlight distinct roles of the plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement. The lithosphere architecture strongly controls the ascent and emplacement of the plume head because the plume lacks sufficient vigor, owing to the impediment of significant stagnant slabs in the mantle transition zone beneath the Leiqiong area. Thus, the Hainan mantle plume initially ascended through a lithospheric weak zone in eastern Leiqiong area and subsequently propagated laterally across the northern Leiqiong area due to lateral variations in lithospheric architecture. The mantle sources of both stages were influenced by the Hainan plume during its ascent and lateral propagation. As the plume expanded, fusible recycled sedimentary components accumulated beneath the lithospheric weak zone, while the surrounding mantle was depleted due to tectonic suction toward the central region. Consequently, late-stage basalts in northwestern Leiqiong primarily record a signature of recycled oceanic crust introduced by the plume. Therefore, the plume governs the compositional evolution of the mantle sources during its propagation. Published: 2026-06-07T14:25:32+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.587 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guo-Hong Sun; Xiao-Long Huang; Fan Yang; Yang Yu; Sheng-Zhu Zhu

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108664

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

The emplacement of plume head is a critical aspect of mantle plume theory; however, the respective roles of the plume and lithosphere during the emplacement remain insufficiently understood. The late Cenozoic Hainan mantle plume, inferred to remain in the plume head stage, preserves records of its emplacement processes. This study presents comprehensive geochemical data, including major and trace elements, as well as Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopes, for late Cenozoic basalts from the northern Leiqiong area (including Leizhou Peninsula, northern Hainan Island, and Weizhou Island), which are associated with the Hainan plume. Based on their spatial-temporal distribution, these basalts are categorized into early and late stages. Early-stage basalts, restricted to eastern Leiqiong area, show positive ΔεHf values (0.51–2.34) and consistently low Ce/Pb (7.22–18.24) and Nb/Th (6.78–10.52) ratios, suggesting a significant contribution from recycled sedimentary components. Late-stage basalts, distributed across the northern Leiqiong area, are subdivided into two groups based on their ΔεHf values. The eastern late-stage basalts display positive ΔεHf values with overall low Ce/Pb and Nb/Th ratios, whereas the western late-stage basalts show negative ΔεHf values (−1.71 to −0.01) with overall higher Ce/Pb (14.59–31.78) and Nb/Th (10.64–12.77) ratios, indicating a substantial contribution of recycled oceanic crust in their mantle source. The spatial-temporal distribution and geochemical variations of the Leiqiong basalts highlight distinct roles of the plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement. The lithosphere architecture strongly controls the ascent and emplacement of the plume head because the plume lacks sufficient vigor, owing to the impediment of significant stagnant slabs in the mantle transition zone beneath the Leiqiong area. Thus, the Hainan mantle plume initially ascended through a lithospheric weak zone in eastern Leiqiong area and subsequently propagated laterally across the northern Leiqiong area due to lateral variations in lithospheric architecture. The mantle sources of both stages were influenced by the Hainan plume during its ascent and lateral propagation. As the plume expanded, fusible recycled sedimentary components accumulated beneath the lithospheric weak zone, while the surrounding mantle was depleted due to tectonic suction toward the central region. Consequently, late-stage basalts in northwestern Leiqiong primarily record a signature of recycled oceanic crust introduced by the plume. Therefore, the plume governs the compositional evolution of the mantle sources during its propagation.

item.title: Fluid pathways, geochemical zonation, and exploration of Carlin-type gold deposits, Youjiang Basin, China: insights from the Lannigou 3D geochemical system
item.dc:creator: Shengtao Cao
Qinping Tan
Zhuojun Xie
Yong Xia
Jianzhong Liu
Yongzhang Zhou
Lujing Zheng
Yumin Lu
Kelin Zhou
Tingxian Ren
Wei Gao
Jingdan Xiao
Ruizhong Hu
item.description: The Youjiang Basin in southwestern China is the world’s second-largest province of Carlin-type gold deposits, yet its exploration potential remains underexplored compared to its counterpart in Nevada, USA. A primary challenge has been the lack of robust, data-driven metallogenic models to guide the search for deeper, concealed orebodies. This study addresses this critical knowledge gap by conducting a comprehensive geochemical investigation of the Lannigou deposit, the largest fault-controlled Carlin-type gold deposit in the basin. In this work, we integrated large, spatially matched geochemical datasets from deep drill-core rock (n = 449) and surface soil (n = 2,280) samples. Through comprehensive statistical analyses, mass transfer calculations, and innovative three-dimensional spatial visualization, we successfully (1) identified distinct ore-related (Au-As-Hg-Sb-Ag-Tl-W-Mo) and diagenetic (Bi, Co, Cu, Ni, Zn, Pb) elemental suites, delineating their controlling geological mechanisms; (2) established a robust vertical geochemical zonation pattern (Mo → Tl → Sb → W → As → Au → Ag → Hg), which serves as a powerful vector for assessing the erosional level of mineralizing systems; and (3) revealed the critical role of a deep-seated, bedding-parallel detachment fault (F1) as a master conduit, channeling ore-forming fluids from basement structures into shallower, ore-hosting reverse faults. Based on these findings, we propose a new metallogenic model wherein ore-forming fluids ascended from depth, migrated laterally along this major detachment fault, and subsequently precipitated gold within structurally favorable traps, such as secondary reverse faults on anticlinal limbs. Our metallogenic model and the identified geochemical vectors offer robust and actionable criteria for targeting concealed Carlin-type orebodies in the Youjiang Basin and analogous terranes worldwide. Published: 2026-06-04T04:14:34+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.582 (consider)

The Youjiang Basin in southwestern China is the world’s second-largest province of Carlin-type gold deposits, yet its exploration potential remains underexplored compared to its counterpart in Nevada, USA. A primary challenge has been the lack of robust, data-driven metallogenic models to guide the search for deeper, concealed orebodies. This study addresses this critical knowledge gap by conducting a comprehensive geochemical investigation of the Lannigou deposit, the largest fault-controlled Carlin-type gold deposit in the basin. In this work, we integrated large, spatially matched geochemical datasets from deep drill-core rock (n = 449) and surface soil (n = 2,280) samples. Through comprehensive statistical analyses, mass transfer calculations, and innovative three-dimensional spatial visualization, we successfully (1) identified distinct ore-related (Au-As-Hg-Sb-Ag-Tl-W-Mo) and diagenetic (Bi, Co, Cu, Ni, Zn, Pb) elemental suites, delineating their controlling geological mechanisms; (2) established a robust vertical geochemical zonation pattern (Mo → Tl → Sb → W → As → Au → Ag → Hg), which serves as a powerful vector for assessing the erosional level of mineralizing systems; and (3) revealed the critical role of a deep-seated, bedding-parallel detachment fault (F1) as a master conduit, channeling ore-forming fluids from basement structures into shallower, ore-hosting reverse faults. Based on these findings, we propose a new metallogenic model wherein ore-forming fluids ascended from depth, migrated laterally along this major detachment fault, and subsequently precipitated gold within structurally favorable traps, such as secondary reverse faults on anticlinal limbs. Our metallogenic model and the identified geochemical vectors offer robust and actionable criteria for targeting concealed Carlin-type orebodies in the Youjiang Basin and analogous terranes worldwide.

item.title: Crustal evolution of the Archean Rae Province (northern Canada) from oxygen and hafnium isotopes in zircon
item.dc:creator: Jamie A. Cutts
William J. Davis
Quinn Emon
Richard A. Stern
item.description: The isotope characteristics of the continental crust are fundamental to understanding its formation and evolution. We present zircon O and Hf isotope data for 115 Archean magmatic rocks (>2.58 Ga) from the Rae Province (northern Canada), providing constraints that have been missing from the global Archean archive thus far. Zircon from >2.65 Ga, dominantly tonalite−trondhjemite−granodiorite samples, show mantle-like δ18O, while εHfinitial and two-stage depleted mantle model ages (TDM) are variable and define four broad isotope domains. Two domains comprise relatively juvenile crust (TDM < 3.0 Ga) that define a central spine of the Rae Province. These are flanked to the west and east by two separate domains of more evolved crust with Meso- to Eoarchean histories (TDM > 3.0 Ga). A decrease in εHfinitial with age within each isotope domain is most consistent with infracrustal reworking of local crust. Relative to pre−2.65 Ga crust, zircon from 2.65−2.58 Ga potassic granitoids show a greater range in δ18O, whereas their εHfinitial are more homogenized. Zircon yielding δ18O >6.5‰ are dominant in the northern Rae Province, where sedimentary sequences are most abundant and likely reflect widespread erosion and sediment/crustal recycling following the emergence of >2.65 Ga crust. The relatively homogeneous εHfinitial is most consistent with an infracrustal origin and mixing with local sediment. The zircon isotope data are used to evaluate and refine boundaries of crustal blocks, and to interrogate models for the construction of the Rae Province. The isotope signatures of the Rae Province are consistent with the global Archean archive for the emergence of the depleted mantle and cratonization. Published: 2026-06-09T20:04:51+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.581 (consider)

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

The isotope characteristics of the continental crust are fundamental to understanding its formation and evolution. Zircon from >2.65 Ga, dominantly tonalite−trondhjemite−granodiorite samples, show mantle-like δ18O, while εHfinitial and two-stage depleted mantle model ages (TDM) are variable and define four broad isotope domains. Two domains comprise relatively juvenile crust (TDM < 3.0 Ga) that define a central spine of the Rae Province. These are flanked to the west and east by two separate domains of more evolved crust with Meso- to Eoarchean histories (TDM > 3.0 Ga). A decrease in εHfinitial with age within each isotope domain is most consistent with infracrustal reworking of local crust. Relative to pre−2.65 Ga crust, zircon from 2.65−2.58 Ga potassic granitoids show a greater range in δ18O, whereas their εHfinitial are more homogenized. The relatively homogeneous εHfinitial is most consistent with an infracrustal origin and mixing with local sediment. The zircon isotope data are used to evaluate and refine boundaries of crustal blocks, and to interrogate models for the construction of the Rae Province. The isotope signatures of the Rae Province are consistent with the global Archean archive for the emergence of the depleted mantle and cratonization.

item.title: Ti-poor high-Al and high-Cr podiform chromitites in the Ngape ophiolite, myanmar: constraints from mineral chemistry and Re-Os isotopes
item.dc:creator: Toe Toe Kyaw Lin
Hong-Fu Zhang
A-Bing Lin
Juan Zhang
item.description: The Ngape ophiolite in western Myanmar preserves critical records of Neo-Tethyan Ocean tectonic evolution. Here, we present the integrated mineral chemistry, whole-rock platinum group element (PGE), and Re-Os isotopic study of podiform chromitites from this ophiolite to constrain their petrogenesis and tectonic setting. The chromitites are classified into high-Al (Cr# = 56.06–60.06) and high-Cr (Cr# = 63.01–72.63) types. Both types are notably poor in TiO2 (<0.29 wt%). Calculated parental melts indicate that the high-Al chromitites crystallized from the tholeiitic melts, whereas the high-Cr chromitites originated from boninitic melts. The chromitites exhibit enrichment of IPGE (Os, Ir, Ru) over PPGE (Pt, Pd, Rh), and possess unradiogenic, homogeneous 187Os/188Os ratios (0.12638–0.12863; average 0.12695). These PGE and Os isotopic signatures point to a depleted mantle source. Trace elements (e.g., V, Sc, Ga) further reveal that the high-Cr chromitites formed under higher oxygen fugacity and from a more depleted source than the high-Al variety. The coexistence of these Ti-poor chromitites records a progressive magmatic evolution within a forearc mantle wedge, transitioning from nascent subduction initiation (high-Al type) to a geochemically mature stage of the subduction initiation sequence (high-Cr type). This study provides the comprehensive Re-Os isotopic dataset for Myanmar ophiolites and documents a complete, in-situ magmatic progression during forearc evolution in the Neo-Tethyan realm. Published: 2026-06-07T14:26:51+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.581 (consider)

The Ngape ophiolite in western Myanmar preserves critical records of Neo-Tethyan Ocean tectonic evolution. Here, we present the integrated mineral chemistry, whole-rock platinum group element (PGE), and Re-Os isotopic study of podiform chromitites from this ophiolite to constrain their petrogenesis and tectonic setting. The chromitites are classified into high-Al (Cr# = 56.06–60.06) and high-Cr (Cr# = 63.01–72.63) types. Both types are notably poor in TiO2 (<0.29 wt%).
